location.origin: Chester, Montana. A small town on the Hi-Line, the northern Montana rail corridor. East of the Cascades, a completely different landscape from the Puget Sound lowlands. The engine's first non-PNW-corridor location. Montana enters at degree 65.
location.formative: Harvard University and New England Conservatory of Music. The most formally trained artist in the engine. Classical piano technique at the highest level, then a return to Montana and folk melody. The Harvard-NEC pipeline is the highest-credentialed in the engine.
location.career: Windham Hill Records (1986-present). High Plains (1986), Out of the Frame (1988), Cinema (1995). Solo piano and ensemble recordings blending classical technique with folk sensibility and Montana landscape impressionism. Connection to Seattle through performances and PNW touring circuit, not residence.

labels: Windham Hill Records (1986-present: the quintessential New Age label, founded by William Ackerman in Palo Alto, CA, 1976; first appearance in the engine; defined the New Age sound: solo piano, acoustic guitar, ambient textures, contemplative atmosphere; roster includes George Winston, Michael Hedges, Liz Story, Alex de Grassi; clean recording, acoustic instruments, no vocals, no drums; the label's aesthetic is solitude, individual instrument, the sound of one musician in one room)

members.name: Philip Aaberg
members.note: Pianist and composer from Chester, Montana. Harvard and New England Conservatory trained. Music blends classical piano technique with folk sensibility and Montana landscape impressionism. The most formally trained artist in the engine. E=1 reflects the piano at the threshold of silence -- every struck note already on its way to disappearing. The open plain as acoustic signature.

associated_musicians.name: George Winston
associated_musicians.note: Solo pianist on Windham Hill Records. December (1982) sold over three million copies. Winston's piano is ambient, impressionistic, seasonally themed. Aaberg's piano is more folk-rooted, landscape-specific. Both on Windham Hill, different approaches to solo piano.

associated_musicians.name: William Ackerman
associated_musicians.note: Guitarist who founded Windham Hill Records in Palo Alto, CA, 1976. The label's aesthetic -- clean acoustic recording, contemplative atmosphere -- was Ackerman's curatorial vision. Aaberg was part of the roster that defined the Windham Hill sound.

associated_musicians.name: Michael Hedges
associated_musicians.note: Acoustic guitarist who reinvented guitar technique. Aerial Boundaries (1984). Hedges and Aaberg were labelmates on Windham Hill, representing guitar and piano branches of the same acoustic vision.

associated_musicians.name: Overton Berry
associated_musicians.note: Jazz pianist, Seattle's Central District. E=1. Self-taught. The late-night cocktail lounge. Aaberg and Berry share E=1 and piano but occupy different traditions: jazz vs New Age/folk, autodidact vs conservatory-trained, Seattle vs Montana. Sixty-degree gap between piano-primary artists.

associated_musicians.name: Patti Bown
associated_musicians.note: Jazz pianist. Last E=1 before the fifty-nine-degree gap. Bown, Berry, and Aaberg form the E=1 piano trio. All three are pianists. The piano is the E=1 instrument.

associated_musicians.name: Jesse Sykes
associated_musicians.note: Dark folk, prairie noir, E=2. Sykes: the dusk, dark, twilight. Aaberg: the open plain, bright, noon. Same landscape, opposite times of day. Sykes brought the prairie to Seattle sonically; Aaberg brings it physically.

theory_nodes.concept: The open plain (sixty-sixth acoustic element -- the acoustic quality of music that sounds like open space, a landscape with no walls, no ceiling, no boundaries; notes ring out into infinite distance; no reverb in the club sense, just natural piano resonance traveling outward and never returning; the acoustic opposite of the dusk at degree 64; the dusk contracts, the open plain expands; E=1 because the space is so vast that the sound cannot fill it)
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- solo piano recordings where the notes ring out into what sounds like infinite distance. The sound travels outward and never returns. The acoustic signature of the Montana high plains.
Contrast with the dusk (degree 64, Sykes) -- atmospheric, reverb-laden, enclosed, dark room. The open plain is clear, dry, unbounded, noon light. The dusk contracts; the open plain expands.
Contrast with the cathedral harmony (degree 55) -- vertical space, voices stacked upward. The open plain is horizontal space, sound traveling outward across flat terrain.
Contrast with the bare wire (degree 60, Gibbard solo) -- stripped-down intimacy. The open plain is stripped-down enormity: everything removed except the piano and the space.
Contrast with the FM sheen (degree 63, Loggins) -- polished for broadcast. The open plain is transparent, letting the natural sound speak.
E=1 because the acoustic space is infinite. The piano plays into the open plain and the notes diminish, never returning. The amplitude is low because the landscape is larger than the sound.
theory_nodes.description: The open plain is the sixty-sixth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the acoustic quality of music that sounds like unbounded open space -- notes ringing into infinite distance, the natural resonance of a piano traveling outward and never returning. The acoustic opposite of the dusk (degree 64). E=1 because the space is so vast the sound cannot fill it.

ancestors: The dusk (degree 64, Sykes) -- enclosed atmospheric space. The open plain is unbounded natural space.
The bare wire (degree 60, Gibbard solo) -- stripped intimacy. The open plain is stripped enormity.
The FM sheen (degree 63, Loggins) -- broadcast polish. The open plain is acoustic transparency.
genealogy.relationship: Sixty-six acoustic elements across sixty-six degrees. The open plain is the engine's first landscape-scale acoustic element. Track element 67 at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: The Montana connection (sixty-sixth artist-city pattern -- the pattern of an artist who lives outside the PNW corridor entirely but enters the engine's gravitational field through performance and cultural exchange; Aaberg lives in Montana and visits Seattle; the inverse of the dark-folk loner: Sykes brought the prairie to Seattle sonically, Aaberg brings the prairie to Seattle physically; first non-PNW-corridor artist-city pattern)
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- lives in Chester, Montana. Enters the Seattle scene through touring and performance. Not a Seattle resident. The engine's first non-PNW-corridor artist.
Contrast with the dark-folk loner (degree 64, Sykes) -- aesthetic departure within the city. The Montana connection is physical distance: the artist is not in the city at all.
Contrast with the Everett Departure (degree 63, Loggins) -- departure from within the PNW corridor. The Montana connection is presence from outside the corridor entirely.
Contrast with the corridor bridge (degree 61, Y La Bamba) -- connects PNW corridor cities. The Montana connection connects a non-PNW location to the corridor.
The Montana connection opens the question: is Seattle 360 about the city or about the gravitational field? Degree 65 suggests the field.
The geographic displacement is reversed from degree 64: Sykes lives in Seattle and dreams of the prairie; Aaberg lives on the prairie and visits Seattle.
theory_nodes.description: The Montana connection is the sixty-sixth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the pattern of an artist outside the PNW corridor who enters the engine's gravitational field through performance. The inverse of the dark-folk loner. First non-PNW-corridor artist-city pattern.

ancestors: The dark-folk loner (degree 64, Sykes) -- aesthetic departure within the city. The Montana connection is physical presence from outside.
The Everett Departure (degree 63, Loggins) -- departure within the corridor. The Montana connection is outside the corridor.
The literary anchor (degree 57, Gibson) -- rooted in PNW landscape. The Montana connection is rooted in non-PNW landscape.
genealogy.relationship: Sixty-six artist-city patterns across sixty-six degrees. First non-PNW-corridor pattern. Track pattern 67 at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the sustain pedal (sixty-fifth deployment; the blue note that sounds when a piano note is held past its natural life, extended by the sustain pedal, lingering in the air, inevitably decaying; the melancholy of impermanence made audible; every sustained piano note is a note in the process of disappearing; the halo of harmonics and overtones that surround the dying note)
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- long tones ringing across open intervals, chords bleeding into each other through sympathetic vibration. The sustain pedal creates a halo of sound around each note.
Contrast with the slow burn (degree 64, Sykes) -- the melancholy of duration, music that takes too long. The sustain pedal is the melancholy of decay: sound already leaving the moment it arrives.
Contrast with the hometown stranger (degree 63, Loggins) -- absence of connection. The sustain pedal is a fading connection.
The sustain pedal connects all three E=1 artists through their shared instrument. Berry's stride sustain, Bown's jazz voicings, Aaberg's landscape resonance -- three uses of the same mechanism.
Every piano note is a journey from sound toward silence. The sustain pedal delays but does not prevent the journey. The blue note sounds in the gap between the note's birth and its death.
theory_nodes.description: The sustain pedal is the sixty-fifth blue note deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the blue note that sounds when a piano note is held past its natural life -- the melancholy of impermanence, the sound in the process of becoming silence. Connects all three E=1 pianists through their shared instrument's unique mechanism.

ancestors: Blue note -- the slow burn (degree 64, Sykes): melancholy of duration. The sustain pedal is melancholy of decay.
Blue note -- the hometown stranger (degree 63, Loggins): absence of connection. The sustain pedal is a fading connection.
All sixty-four prior blue note deployments (degrees 0-64)
genealogy.relationship: Sixty-five deployments across sixty-six degrees. First piano-specific blue note. Connects E=1 piano trio. Track deployment 66 at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: New Age/Folk at Genre Stage 47 -- first New Age classification in the engine; the hybrid acknowledges Aaberg's folk roots alongside the Windham Hill framework; New Age is quieter than folk -- the genre's structural commitment to contemplation pulls energy below E=2 to E=1; Windham Hill defined the New Age sound; 'New Age' label controversial, Aaberg prefers 'American piano music' or 'Montana music'
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- New Age/Folk. First New Age classification. Genre Stage 47.

Genre progression: Folk (64), New Age/Folk (65). The hybrid returns after pure folk, but this hybrid is different from 61-62-63: those combined folk with indie/soft rock. This combines folk with New Age -- a new genre frontier.
New Age pulls energy below the folk floor: pure folk = E=2 (three data points). New Age/Folk = E=1. The New Age element is what makes E=1 possible.
Windham Hill Records defined the New Age sound. The label's house style: clean recording, acoustic instruments, no vocals, contemplative atmosphere.
Genre legitimacy question: New Age is the only genre in the engine that remains critically marginalized. The engine classifies without judging.
Aaberg resists the New Age label, preferring 'American piano music.' The slash in New Age/Folk acknowledges that the music exceeds the label.
theory_nodes.description: New Age/Folk at degree 65 establishes Genre Stage 47 -- the first New Age classification in the engine. The hybrid acknowledges Aaberg's folk roots alongside the Windham Hill New Age framework. The New Age element pulls energy below the folk floor (E=2) to E=1. Windham Hill Records defined the genre's sonic identity.

ancestors: Folk at degree 64 (Sykes) -- Genre Stage 46. Pure folk. E=2. New Age/Folk drops below.
Folk at degree 60 (Gibbard solo) -- Genre Stage 42. Pure folk. E=2.
No prior New Age appearance. First New Age genre entry.
genealogy.relationship: Genre Stage 47 at degree 65. First New Age classification. New Age/Folk hybrid. New Age pulls energy below folk floor. Track genre at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: E=1 returns at degree 65 -- third E=1 deployment; fifty-nine-degree gap since last E=1 (Patti Bown at degree 6); longest absence of any energy level; distribution 3:21:15:16:10:2; all three E=1 artists are pianists (Berry 5, Bown 6, Aaberg 65); the piano is the E=1 instrument; E=1 is piano territory
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- E=1. Third E=1 ever. Fifty-nine-degree gap since the last E=1.
E=1 piano trio: Berry (5, jazz), Bown (6, jazz), Aaberg (65, New Age/Folk). All pianists. No other instrument has reached E=1.
Energy distribution after degree 65: E1:3, E2:21, E3:15, E4:16, E5:10, E6:2. E=1 rises from 2 to 3. Tied with E=6 as rarest.
The piano's natural decay makes E=1 possible: every struck note diminishes toward silence. The sustain pedal delays but does not prevent. E=1 is the instrument playing into its own fade.
E=1 is below folk (E=2). The New Age element in New Age/Folk is what pulls the energy below the folk floor.
Berry's E=1: the quiet of a late-night piano bar. Aaberg's E=1: the quiet of the Montana high plains. Different silences, same threshold.
theory_nodes.description: E=1 at degree 65 is the third E=1 deployment in the Seattle 360 engine, returning after a fifty-nine-degree gap (the longest absence of any energy level). Energy distribution: E1:3, E2:21, E3:15, E4:16, E5:10, E6:2. All three E=1 artists are pianists. The piano is the E=1 instrument -- its natural decay, wide dynamic range, and sustain pedal make it the only instrument capable of operating at the threshold of silence.

ancestors: E=2 at degree 64 (Sykes): 21st deployment. E=1 drops below E=2.
E=1 at degree 6 (Bown): 2nd deployment. Last E=1 before the gap.
E=1 at degree 5 (Berry): 1st deployment. Piano-E=1 correlation established.
genealogy.relationship: Three E=1 deployments across sixty-six degrees. All pianists. E=1 = piano. Track at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: Montana enters -- first non-PNW-corridor location in the engine; Chester, Montana, on the Hi-Line; east of the Cascades, a different landscape, different climate, different world; the engine's geographic boundary breaks; the hi-line is high plains: flat, dry, windswept, treeless, the opposite of the Puget Sound lowlands
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- Chester, Montana. First non-PNW-corridor location. East of the Cascades.
Prior locations: Seattle, Portland, Bellingham, Everett -- all on the I-5 corridor. Montana is outside the corridor entirely.
Chester on the Hi-Line: flat, dry, windswept, treeless. The opposite of Seattle's green, wet, overcast landscape.
The engine's geographic boundary breaks: Seattle 360 is not strictly about Seattle artists but about the gravitational field centered on Seattle.
Aaberg's connection to Seattle is through performance, not residence. Montana artists perform in Seattle through the PNW touring circuit.
The landscape is audible: sustained piano notes, open intervals, pauses that let sound decay into silence -- the sonic equivalent of the Montana high plains.
theory_nodes.description: Montana enters the engine at degree 65 -- the first non-PNW-corridor location. Chester, Montana, on the Hi-Line, east of the Cascades. The engine's geographic boundary breaks. Seattle 360 is revealed as a gravitational field, not a city boundary. The Montana high plains landscape is audible in Aaberg's music.

ancestors: Everett at degree 63 (Loggins) -- PNW corridor edge. Montana is beyond the corridor.
Seattle (broad) at degree 64 (Sykes) -- returned to center. Montana departs from center entirely.
Portland at degree 57 (Gibson) -- PNW corridor city. Montana is outside the corridor.
genealogy.relationship: First non-PNW-corridor location at degree 65. Geographic boundary breaks. Track geography at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: Windham Hill Records enters -- the quintessential New Age label, founded by William Ackerman in Palo Alto 1976; defined the New Age sound; roster: George Winston, Michael Hedges, Liz Story, Alex de Grassi, Philip Aaberg; clean acoustic recording, contemplative atmosphere; George Winston's December sold 3+ million copies; first appearance in the engine
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- Windham Hill Records. High Plains (1986) on Windham Hill.
Windham Hill roster: Winston (piano), Hedges (guitar), Story (piano), de Grassi (guitar), Aaberg (piano). Acoustic virtuosos.
Label aesthetic: clean recording, acoustic instruments, no vocals, no drums, contemplative atmosphere. The sound of one musician in one room.
George Winston's December (1982) -- 3+ million copies. Proved mass audience for contemplative instrumental music.
Contrast with Barsuk Records (57-64): Barsuk curates across genres (folk to indie rock). Windham Hill curates within a single aesthetic (acoustic contemplation).
Contrast with indie rock labels: indie is about energy, community, the band. Windham Hill is about solitude, the individual instrument, the sound of one musician.
theory_nodes.description: Windham Hill Records enters the engine at degree 65 -- the quintessential New Age label. Founded by William Ackerman in 1976. Defined the New Age sound with clean acoustic recording and contemplative atmosphere. Roster included George Winston, Michael Hedges, and Philip Aaberg. First appearance in the engine.

ancestors: Barsuk Records at degree 64 (Sykes) -- most recent label. Independent label with curatorial identity. Windham Hill is a different curatorial model.
No prior New Age label appearance. First New Age label.
genealogy.relationship: Windham Hill Records at degree 65. First New Age label. First appearance. Track label at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: Piano-primary artist returns -- first piano-focused artist since Overton Berry at degree 5; sixty-degree gap; all three E=1 artists are pianists; the piano's natural decay (struck string diminishes toward silence), sustain pedal (delays but does not prevent silence), and wide dynamic range make it the E=1 instrument; the piano at E=1 is the instrument most comfortable with its own disappearance
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- piano-primary artist. First since Overton Berry (degree 5). Sixty-degree gap.
Piano's unique acoustic properties: struck string decays naturally. Sustain pedal delays but does not prevent silence. The piano's natural behavior is to fade.
At E=1, the pianist plays into the fade -- striking notes that are already on their way to silence. The instrument is comfortable with its own disappearance.
Aaberg's classical training (Harvard, NEC) gives him the technical control to play at the very bottom of the dynamic range -- keys struck so softly the hammer barely touches the string.
Berry (degree 5): stride/cocktail piano, jazz, self-taught. Aaberg (degree 65): New Age/folk piano, classical training. Same instrument, different traditions, sixty degrees apart.
Guitar can be quiet (E=2) but not E=1. Voice can be quiet (E=2) but not E=1. Only the piano reaches E=1.
theory_nodes.description: Piano-primary artist returns at degree 65 after a sixty-degree absence (since Berry at degree 5). The piano's unique acoustic properties -- natural decay, sustain pedal, wide dynamic range -- make it the only instrument that reaches E=1. All three E=1 artists are pianists. The piano at E=1 is the instrument most comfortable with its own disappearance.

ancestors: Overton Berry at degree 5 -- first piano-primary artist. Jazz, stride, self-taught.
Patti Bown at degree 6 -- second piano artist. Jazz, bebop-inflected.
No piano-primary artist from degrees 7-64. Sixty-degree gap.
genealogy.relationship: Piano returns at degree 65 after sixty degrees absent. E=1-piano correlation 100%. Track piano at future degrees.

theory_nodes.concept: Classical training -- Harvard and New England Conservatory; most formally trained artist in the engine; educational pipelines: Garfield (Jones), UW (Standifer), self-taught (Berry), Berklee (Frisell), Harvard/NEC (Aaberg); elite training applied to populist genre; overqualification as artistic advantage
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- Harvard University and New England Conservatory. Highest-credentialed artist in the engine.
Educational pipelines: Garfield (Jones, degree 0), UW (Standifer, degree 4), self-taught (Berry, degree 5), Berklee (Frisell, degree 1), Harvard/NEC (Aaberg, degree 65).
The Harvard-NEC path applied to Windham Hill / New Age -- elite training in service of a populist genre. Overqualification as artistic advantage.
Jones (Berklee, European study) became a polymath producer. Frisell (Berklee) became an experimental guitarist. Aaberg (Harvard/NEC) became a landscape pianist. Training does not determine outcome.
Aaberg could play Chopin and Rachmaninoff. He chose Montana fiddle tunes and landscape impressionism. The choice is the story.
NEC is one of the oldest and most prestigious music schools in the US. Harvard added intellectual breadth. Together they produced a musician of exceptional range.
theory_nodes.description: Philip Aaberg at degree 65 is the most formally trained artist in the engine -- Harvard University and New England Conservatory. The elite training was applied to a populist genre (New Age/folk piano), not the concert hall tradition the academy would have predicted. Overqualification as artistic advantage -- the pattern shared with Quincy Jones (degree 0).

ancestors: Quincy Jones at degree 0 -- Berklee, European study. Elite training applied to pop/film. Same pattern of overqualification.
Bill Frisell at degree 1 -- Berklee. Elite training applied to experimental jazz.
Overton Berry at degree 5 -- self-taught. The opposite educational pipeline.
genealogy.relationship: Highest-credentialed artist at degree 65. Harvard/NEC pipeline new to engine. Training does not determine outcome. Track education at future degrees.

theory_nodes.concept: Prairie diptych: degrees 64-65 -- Sykes (prairie noir, dark, twilight, E=2) and Aaberg (prairie impressionism, bright, noon, E=1); same landscape, opposite times of day; the dusk and the open plain as acoustic inversions of the same geography; Sykes brought the prairie to Seattle sonically, Aaberg brings it physically
examples: Degree 64 (Sykes): prairie noir -- dark, atmospheric, twilight. The dusk. E=2. Folk. Seattle resident.
Degree 65 (Aaberg): prairie impressionism -- bright, clear, noon. The open plain. E=1. New Age/Folk. Montana resident.
Same landscape (the American prairie/high plains), opposite registers: dark vs light, twilight vs noon, E=2 vs E=1.
Sykes lives in Seattle and makes music that sounds like the prairie. Aaberg lives on the prairie and brings it to Seattle.
The dusk contracts (reverb, atmosphere, enclosure). The open plain expands (clarity, space, unbounded).
Consecutive degrees at the same latitude but different longitudes: Seattle and Montana, both touching the prairie, from opposite sides.
theory_nodes.description: Degrees 64-65 form a prairie diptych: Jesse Sykes' prairie noir (dark, twilight, E=2) and Philip Aaberg's prairie impressionism (bright, noon, E=1). Same landscape, opposite times of day. The dusk and the open plain as acoustic inversions. The displacement is also reversed: Sykes brought the prairie to Seattle sonically; Aaberg brings it physically.

ancestors: The dusk at degree 64 (Sykes) -- the dark half of the diptych.
Prairie noir at degree 64 -- the dark aesthetic. Prairie impressionism at degree 65 is the light aesthetic.
The FM sheen-to-dusk inversion (63-64) -- consecutive-degree acoustic contrast continues at 64-65.
genealogy.relationship: Consecutive-degree landscape diptych. Prairie at dusk (64) and noon (65). Track consecutive contrasts at future degrees.

theory_nodes.concept: Territorial acknowledgment extends to Montana -- Blackfeet and northern Plains nations; Chester, Montana, near the Blackfeet Reservation; the engine's territorial awareness extends beyond the Coast Salish framework for the first time; when performing in Seattle, Duwamish territory; when at home in Montana, Blackfeet territory
examples: Degree 65 -- Chester, Montana. Near the Blackfeet Reservation. The engine's territorial acknowledgment extends beyond the Coast Salish cultural area.
Prior territories: Duwamish (Seattle), Multnomah (Portland), Lummi/Nooksack (Bellingham), Snohomish (Everett). All Coast Salish.
Montana's indigenous territories include Blackfeet, Assiniboine, Gros Ventre, and other nations.
Dual territorial context: Duwamish when performing in Seattle, Blackfeet when at home in Montana.
The territorial extension mirrors the geographic extension: Montana entering the engine extends both the geographic and territorial maps.
theory_nodes.description: The territorial acknowledgment at degree 65 extends beyond the Coast Salish cultural area for the first time, recognizing the Blackfeet and other northern Plains nations whose territory includes Chester, Montana. The engine's territorial map now spans from the Puget Sound to the Montana high plains.

ancestors: Territorial acknowledgment at degree 64 (Seattle/Duwamish) -- returned to center. Degree 65 extends beyond.
Territorial acknowledgment at degree 63 (Everett/Snohomish) -- most recent extension within PNW. Degree 65 extends beyond PNW.
genealogy.relationship: Territorial acknowledgment extends to Montana at degree 65. First non-Coast-Salish territory. Track at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: High Plains (1986, Windham Hill) -- debut album; solo piano mapping the Montana landscape onto the keyboard; sustained notes held into silence mimicking how sound carries across open prairie; the high-plains music that established Aaberg's aesthetic; the title is literal: high-plains music from the high plains
examples: High Plains (1986) -- the debut on Windham Hill. Solo piano. The aesthetic announced in the title.
The sustain pedal holds notes into silence, mimicking the way sound carries across open prairie -- traveling farther than it should, then disappearing.
The title is literal: Aaberg is from the high plains of Montana. The music is the high plains rendered as piano composition.
Positioning within Windham Hill: alongside George Winston's seasonal albums and Michael Hedges' guitar innovations. Aaberg's contribution: landscape-specific piano.
The debut established Aaberg's aesthetic fully formed -- like Sykes' Reckless Burning (degree 64), the vision was complete from the first album.
theory_nodes.description: High Plains (1986, Windham Hill Records) is Philip Aaberg's debut album -- solo piano mapping the Montana landscape onto the keyboard. The title is literal: high-plains music from the high plains. Sustained notes held into silence, mimicking how sound carries across open prairie. The aesthetic was fully formed at the debut.

ancestors: Windham Hill Records catalog -- the label's acoustic-contemplative framework.
Montana folk tradition -- fiddle tunes, hymns, the musical vocabulary of the American West.
genealogy.relationship: Debut album. The aesthetic thesis statement. Sets the trajectory for Out of the Frame (1988) and Cinema (1995).

theory_nodes.concept: Gender: male -- no gender ratio change; ratio remains 19:46 (non-male:total) through degree 64; at degree 65, 19:47 (~40.4% male, ~29.1% non-male factoring Aaberg as male); male continues after Sykes broke the two-peat at 64
examples: Philip Aaberg (degree 65) -- male. Gender ratio: 19:47 (non-male:total), approximately 40.4%.
Gender pattern: non-male (64, Sykes), male (65, Aaberg). The single non-male at 64 did not start a new non-male run.
Ratio dips slightly from 29.2% (19/65) to approximately 28.8% (19/66).
Male artists from the recent stretch: Pickwick (62), Loggins (63), Aaberg (65). Sykes (64) as single non-male interruption.
theory_nodes.description: Philip Aaberg at degree 65 is male. Gender ratio adjusts to 19:47 (non-male:total), approximately 28.8%. The single non-male appearance at degree 64 (Sykes) did not begin a sustained non-male run. Track at degree 66.

ancestors: Non-male at degree 64 (Sykes): broke the male two-peat at 62-63. Single interruption.
Male at degree 63 (Loggins): male two-peat.
genealogy.relationship: Gender ratio 19:47 (~28.8%) at degree 65. Male. Track at degree 66.

theory_nodes.concept: The New Age question: genre legitimacy and the Windham Hill problem -- New Age dismissed as background music but its musicians were virtuosos with compositional ambition; the 'New Age' label applied from outside, not chosen from within; Aaberg prefers 'American piano music'; the only genre in the engine that remains critically marginalized
examples: New Age genre history: Grammy category created 1987. Term used derisively by critics. Dismissed as 'elevator music with better production.'
Windham Hill's roster included genuine virtuosos: Hedges reinvented guitar technique, Winston sold millions, Aaberg brought conservatory training.
The music was used as background (yoga studios, bookstores) but the compositions were foreground: serious engagements with acoustic beauty.
Aaberg resists the label, preferring 'American piano music' or 'Montana music.' The term was applied from outside.
New Age is the only genre in the engine that remains critically marginalized -- still dismissed, still awaiting reappraisal.
The engine classifies without judging. New Age/Folk at Genre Stage 47 because the genre history is part of the story.
theory_nodes.description: The arrival of New Age at Genre Stage 47 raises the genre legitimacy question. New Age was dismissed by critics but its musicians were virtuosos. The label was applied from outside, not chosen from within. Aaberg prefers 'American piano music.' New Age is the only genre in the engine that remains critically marginalized.

ancestors: No prior New Age discussion in the engine. First engagement with the genre.
The genre hierarchy question has been implicit since the engine began. New Age makes it explicit.
genealogy.relationship: New Age enters at degree 65. Genre legitimacy question opened. Track critical reception patterns at future degrees.

theory_nodes.concept: Dusk to open plain: degrees 64-65 as acoustic inversion -- Sykes' enclosed atmospheric intimacy to Aaberg's unbounded landscape transparency; the dusk contracts, the open plain expands; E=2 to E=1; reverb-laden to acoustically clear; dark room to treeless horizon; consecutive degrees at opposite poles of the spatial spectrum
examples: Degree 64 (Sykes, E=2): the dusk -- atmospheric, reverb-laden, enclosed. Music for one listener in a dark room.
Degree 65 (Aaberg, E=1): the open plain -- transparent, clear, unbounded. Music that exists whether anyone hears it or not.
The dusk requires the listener to come to it. The open plain does not need the listener at all.
E=2 to E=1 -- the energy drops one level. From the threshold of audibility to the threshold of silence.
Three consecutive acoustic inversions: FM sheen to dusk (63-64), dusk to open plain (64-65). Each degree the acoustic opposite of the last.
Reverb-laden to acoustically clear. Indoor space to outdoor space. Atmospheric to transparent.
theory_nodes.description: Degrees 64-65 present an acoustic inversion: the dusk (Sykes, atmospheric intimacy, E=2) to the open plain (Aaberg, landscape transparency, E=1). Consecutive degrees at opposite poles of the spatial spectrum. The dusk contracts; the open plain expands. Three consecutive acoustic inversions now: FM sheen to dusk to open plain.

ancestors: The dusk at degree 64 (Sykes) -- enclosed atmospheric space. The open plain is unbounded natural space.
The FM sheen-to-dusk at degrees 63-64 -- previous consecutive-degree inversion. The pattern continues.
genealogy.relationship: Consecutive-degree acoustic inversion. Dusk to open plain. Three inversions in a row (63-64-65). Track consecutive contrasts at future degrees.

cross_references.relationship: E=1 shared, piano shared. Berry: self-taught jazz pianist, Seattle's Central District, the late-night cocktail lounge. Aaberg: Harvard/NEC-trained New Age/folk pianist, Montana, the open prairie. Same energy, same instrument, different traditions. Sixty-degree gap between piano-primary artists. E=1-piano correlation 100%.
E=1 shared, piano shared. Last E=1 before the fifty-nine-degree gap. Three E=1 artists form the piano trio: Berry (5), Bown (6), Aaberg (65). All pianists. The piano is the E=1 instrument.

Prairie shared, acoustic inversion. Sykes: prairie noir, dark, twilight, E=2. Aaberg: prairie impressionism, bright, noon, E=1. Same landscape, opposite times of day. The dusk and the open plain. Sykes brought the prairie to Seattle sonically; Aaberg brings it physically. Consecutive-degree diptych.

Quiet introspection shared. Gibbard: E=2, voice and guitar, the bare wire. Aaberg: E=1, solo piano, the open plain. Both quiet. Aaberg is quieter. The difference between whispering and holding your breath.

Classical training connection. Jones: Berklee, European study, polymath producer. Aaberg: Harvard, NEC, landscape pianist. Both applied elite training to genres the academy did not prioritize. Overqualification as artistic advantage.
